summary: This lesson introduces 'elegir' (to choose) with its e->i stem change, teaches the subjunctive after 'para que' (so that/in order that), introduces 'volver' (to return) with its o->ue stem change, the past participle 'hecho' (done/made), and 'también' (also/as well).

teachings.conceptName: E to I stem change (elegir type)
teachings.summary: Some verbs like elegir have an e that turns into i (rather than splitting to ie) when stressed. Elijo (I choose), but elegimos (we choose -- unstressed, no change).
teachings.rule: When the stem e is stressed, it becomes i (not ie). When unstressed, it stays as e. Example: elijo, eliges, elige, elegimos, eligen.

examples.english: I choose.
You choose. (informal)
We choose.

examples.spanish: No quiero que elijas por mí.
examples.english: I don't want you to choose for me (on my behalf).

teachings.summary: After 'para que' (so that / in order that), the subjunctive is obligatory. Note: 'para + infinitive' (in order to) does NOT use the subjunctive. The subjunctive is needed only when there is a 'que' and a different subject.
teachings.rule: para que + [subjunctive]. But para + [infinitive] = in order to (no subjunctive).

examples.english: We have cooked so that they eat.
We have met up in order to speak. (no que, no subjunctive)

examples.spanish: Lo he hecho para que vuelvas.
examples.english: I have done it so that you come back. (informal)

examples.spanish: Lo he hecho para que vuelvan.
examples.english: I have done it so that they come back.

examples.spanish: Lo han hecho para que volvamos.
examples.english: They have done it so that we come back.

examples.spanish: Hemos hecho todo para que tú también vuelvas.
examples.english: We have done everything so that you also come back.

teachings.notes: Para can be shortened to 'pa' in some dialects: 'pa que volvamos'.

teachings.conceptName: Volver (to return / to go back / to come back)
teachings.summary: Volver means to return/go back/come back. The o splits to ue when stressed (vuelvo, vuelves). Related to 'revolve'; 'revolver' in Spanish means to stir.

examples.english: I return / I come back.
You return. (informal)
So that you come back.
So that they come back.
So that we come back.

teachings.notes: Related to 'revolve' in English. In Spanish, 'revolver' means to stir.

teachings.summary: Romper is to break (transitive); romperse is to break (intransitive -- something breaks by itself). Practiced with the subjunctive: 'no quiero que se rompa' (I don't want it to break).

examples.english: I don't want it to break.
I don't want him/her to break it.

teachings.notes: Related to rapture, corrupt, disrupt, erupt -- the -rupt root.

teachings.summary: Hecho is the irregular past participle of hacer. Also means 'fact' as a noun.

examples.english: I have done.

examples.spanish: Lo he hecho para que vuelvas.
examples.english: I have done it so that you come back.
We have done everything.

teachings.summary: También means 'also' or 'as well'. It contains 'tan' (so/as) + 'bien' (well), making it a near-direct translation of the English 'as well'. The n of tan becomes m before the b.

examples.english: So that you too come back.

examples.spanish: Hemos hecho todo para que tú también vuelvas.
examples.english: We have done everything so that you also come back.

teachings.notes: también = tan (as/so) + bien (well). The n softened to m before the b. A near-direct translation of English 'as well'.

teachings.summary: Encontrarse (reflexive of encontrar) means 'to meet up' -- literally 'to find oneself/each other'.

examples.english: We have met up to speak.

vocabulary.english: to return / to go back / to come back
vocabulary.derivation: Related to 'revolve'; stem change o -> ue
